proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, multi-observable constraint on the neutron skin thickness from collider data, directly contributing to the symmetry energy equation of state. It will establish a methodology for using combined soft-hadronic observables to disentangle subtle initial-state nuclear structure effects, moving beyond single-observable analyses.
proposals.novelty: While flow ratios and mean p_T have been studied separately for structure, their combined use as correlated, system-dependent constraints to specifically isolate the skin from the radius is a novel synthesis. This leverages the fact that ⟨p_T⟩ ratios are sensitive to overall system size and density, while flow ratios are more sensitive to shape details, providing complementary handles.

proposals.title: Probing Nuclear Structure and Initial-State Geometry in pA and dA Collisions with Isobar Targets via Flow and Fluctuation Observables
proposals.motivation: High-energy pA and dA collisions are crucial for isolating initial-state geometry and nuclear structure effects from the dominant collective dynamics of AA collisions. Prior to 2026, studies of isobar collisions (e.g., Ru+Ru vs. Zr+Zr) in AA systems demonstrated sensitivity to subtle nuclear structure differences. Scaling this approach down to asymmetric pA/dA collisions with isobar targets (e.g., p/d + 96Ru vs. p/d + 96Zr) provides a cleaner probe, where the projectile geometry is simple and well-defined, allowing the target's deformation and surface structure to be imprinted more directly on final-state observables.
proposals.hypothesis: The anisotropic flow coefficients (v_n) and their event-by-event fluctuations in high-energy pA and dA collisions are sensitive to the quadrupole and octupole deformations, as well as the neutron skin, of the isobaric target nuclei, with the deuteron's weak binding providing an additional handle on sub-nucleonic fluctuations.
proposals.method: We will analyze simulated events from a hybrid model combining a Monte Carlo Glauber initial condition with explicit nuclear density profiles for isobar pairs (e.g., 96Ru/96Zr, 44Ca/48Ca). The model will incorporate realistic deformation parameters (β2, β3) from pre-2026 low-energy measurements. We will compute flow vectors and cumulants (e.g., v2{2}, v2{4}, v3{2}) from the azimuthal particle distributions, focusing on the ratio of observables between the two isobar targets. A key analysis will be the comparison of symmetric cumulants SC(m,n) and normalized fluctuation measures ⟨δv_n^2⟩/⟨v_n⟩^2 between pA and dA systems to disentangle target structure from projectile-driven fluctuations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative framework for using small system isobar collisions as a precision tool for nuclear structure. It will provide specific predictions for ongoing and future RHIC and LHC runs, guiding experimental analyses to extract deformation parameters in unstable nuclei. The study will also clarify the role of initial-state vs. final-state effects in the observed collectivity of small systems.
proposals.novelty: While isobar comparisons are established in AA collisions, applying this method to pA/dA systems is novel, offering a direct probe of the target nucleus with minimal confounding final-state effects. Furthermore, using the deuteron as a weakly-bound neutron-proton dimer provides a unique probe of neutron skin and sub-nucleonic geometry not accessible with a proton projectile.

proposals.title: Probing Nuclear Size and Shape in Ultra-Central Collisions via a Ratio of Flow Cumulants
proposals.motivation: In high-energy nuclear collisions, collective flow is a key probe of the initial-state geometry and nuclear structure. However, disentangling the effects of nuclear size, shape, and sub-nucleonic fluctuations is challenging, especially in small systems. Ultra-central collisions, where impact parameter fluctuations are minimized, offer a cleaner environment to isolate the intrinsic nuclear geometry. A method to quantify pure size differences between nuclei is needed to complement existing deformation studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying a ratio method to higher-order flow cumulants (e.g., $v_2\{4\}/v_2\{2\}$) in ultra-central collisions of different symmetric species (e.g., $^{96}$Ru/$^{96}$Zr, $^{208}$Pb/$^{197}$Au), we can isolate a scaling factor directly related to the pure transverse size difference, largely independent of deformation and granularity-driven fluctuations.
proposals.method: We will analyze Pb-Pb, Ru-Ru, Zr-Zr, Xe-Xe, and Au-Au data from the LHC and RHIC. For each system, we will calculate the second- and fourth-order elliptic flow cumulants, $v_2\{2\}$ and $v_2\{4\}$, in ultra-central centrality classes (e.g., 0-0.5%). The ratio $R = v_2\{4\} / v_2\{2\}$ will be computed for each collision system. We will then construct double ratios, e.g., $(R_{\mathrm{RuRu}} / R_{\mathrm{ZrZr}})$, and compare them to predictions from hydrodynamic simulations with Glauber and TRENTo initial conditions, where nuclear parameters (size, deformation, nucleon width) are varied independently.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, robust observable to extract the intrinsic transverse size of atomic nuclei from collider data. It will provide a quantitative baseline for nuclear geometry, against which the signatures of deformation and sub-nucleonic structure in small-system flow can be more clearly interpreted. The results will directly inform global Bayesian analyses constraining initial-state models.
proposals.novelty: While flow ratios have been used to study deformation, applying them specifically to ultra-central collisions to isolate a pure size scaling factor is a novel strategy. This extends the researcher's previous work on deformation probes by providing a complementary, calibration-like measurement for the fundamental geometric scale.

proposals.title: Beyond Geometric Scaling: Testing Nuclear-Structure-Dependent Hydrodynamic Response in Collisions of Isobars
proposals.motivation: In high-energy nuclear collisions, the collective flow of final-state particles is typically understood as a hydrodynamic response to the initial-state geometry. A foundational assumption in state-of-the-art models is that the hydrodynamic transport coefficients (e.g., η/s) and the medium's response are universal, independent of the specific colliding nuclei for a given collision energy and centrality. This assumption is implicitly tested when comparing flow observables between systems like Ru+Ru and Zr+Zr, where differences are attributed solely to their differing initial geometries from nuclear structure. However, it remains an untested conjecture whether intrinsic nuclear structure features (e.g., deformation, skin thickness, alpha-clustering) could themselves modify the local properties or dynamical evolution of the created quark-gluon plasma, thereby breaking this identical-response paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that subtle differences in the internal nucleonic structure of isobaric nuclei (e.g., ⁹⁶Ru+⁹⁶Ru vs. ⁹⁶Zr+⁹⁶Zr) induce modifications in the early-time energy deposition and subsequent medium evolution that cannot be fully captured by a simple geometric scaling of the initial state, leading to measurable deviations in flow observables beyond what is predicted by state-of-the-art models assuming identical hydrodynamic response.
proposals.method: We will perform a high-precision, multi-observable analysis using existing and generated heavy-ion collision data for ⁹⁶Ru+⁹⁶Ru and ⁹⁶Zr+⁹⁶Zr at √s_NN = 200 GeV. The core methodology involves constructing a comprehensive Bayesian framework where state-of-the-art initial-condition models (e.g., TRENTo) are coupled to viscous hydrodynamic evolution (e.g., MUSIC). Crucially, we will explicitly allow key hydrodynamic parameters (e.g., specific shear viscosity η/s, bulk viscosity, initial thermalization time) to vary independently for each isobar system within the Bayesian inference. We will calibrate the model against a suite of flow observables, including v_n{Ψ_m} cumulants and non-linear flow modes, and rigorously compare the posterior distributions of the hydrodynamic parameters for Ru+Ru and Zr+Zr. A statistically significant difference in the posteriors would constitute evidence for a structure-dependent response.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative test of the 'identical hydrodynamic response' assumption for near-identical nuclear species. It will establish whether nuclear structure leaves an imprint on the dynamical properties of the QGP itself, moving beyond its role as a geometric initial condition. A positive finding would necessitate a paradigm shift in how initial conditions are linked to medium evolution, while a null result would significantly strengthen the foundation of current hydrodynamic modeling.
proposals.novelty: While previous isobar studies focused on isolating geometric structure effects, the novel aspect here is to intentionally relax the core modeling assumption of identical medium response and directly search for evidence of its violation, treating the hydrodynamic parameters themselves as probes of nuclear structure.

proposals.title: Probing the Limits of Nuclear Deformation: Flow Decorrelations in Ultra-Peripheral and Central Uranium-Uranium Collisions at RHIC
proposals.motivation: The strong collective flow observed in small collision systems challenges our understanding of initial-state geometry and its hydrodynamic response. While $^{238}$U nuclei possess a large, well-characterized quadrupole deformation, their octupole deformation and the interplay between these shapes in collisions remain poorly constrained. U+U collisions provide a unique, maximally deformed system to test the scaling of initial-state geometry with deformation, a critical input for extracting transport properties of the quark-gluon plasma.
proposals.hypothesis: The magnitude and sign of forward-backward flow vector decorrelations, quantified by $r_n$ correlators, will exhibit a distinct, non-monotonic dependence on collision centrality in U+U collisions, directly mapping the transition from ultra-peripheral collisions dominated by the tip-to-tip nuclear orientation to central collisions dominated by the body-to-body orientation.
proposals.method: We will apply the established flow decorrelation analysis, calculating $r_n(\eta) = \langle \vec{q}_n(-\eta) \cdot \vec{q}_n^*(\eta) \rangle / \langle \vec{q}_n(\eta) \cdot \vec{q}_n^*(\eta) \rangle$, to U+U data from the STAR experiment at RHIC. This will be performed differentially in centrality and for harmonics $n=2,3,4$. The results will be compared to state-of-the-art hydrodynamic model calculations (e.g., MUSIC+IP-Glasma) with explicit implementation of $^{238}$U quadrupole ($\beta_2$) and octupole ($\beta_3$) deformation. A Bayesian analysis will be employed to constrain the $\beta_2$ and $\beta_3$ parameters and their mutual orientation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental constraints on the octupole deformation of $^{238}$U via collective flow in high-energy collisions. It will establish a new, stringent test for initial-state models by isolating the geometric scaling of decorrelations with extreme nuclear deformation. The results will clarify the role of sub-nucleonic fluctuations versus global nuclear shape in generating longitudinal flow structures.
proposals.novelty: While flow decorrelations have been studied in spherical nuclei, applying this sensitive probe to the uniquely deformed U+U system to simultaneously map quadrupole and octupole shape effects is a novel and powerful approach. This moves beyond mean $v_n$ measurements to exploit more differential observables sensitive to the full 3D initial geometry.

proposals.title: Probing the Onset of Collectivity and Nuclear Structure in O+O Collisions via Flow and Fluctuation Measurements
proposals.motivation: High-energy collisions of light ions like oxygen (O+O) are a crucial frontier for understanding the formation of quark-gluon plasma (QGP) in small systems. Pre-2026, theoretical frameworks suggest O+O collisions may exhibit collective flow signatures intermediate between proton-proton and larger nucleus-nucleus collisions, offering a unique probe to disentangle initial-state nuclear structure effects from final-state QGP-driven collectivity. The imminent availability of experimental O+O data from RHIC and/or the LHC provides a critical opportunity for direct validation of these models.
proposals.hypothesis: The measured flow harmonics (v_n) and their event-by-event fluctuations in O+O collisions will be quantitatively described by a framework that explicitly incorporates the quadrupole deformation of 16O, thereby isolating the contribution of initial geometric structure to the observed collectivity and constraining the role of final-state hydrodynamic response.
proposals.method: We will apply the established framework from Jia's recent work, which uses the Glauber model with explicit nuclear density distributions, including 16O's known quadrupole deformation. We will compute initial-state eccentricities (ε_n) and their fluctuations, then propagate them through a relativistic viscous hydrodynamic model (e.g., VISH2+1 or a 3D-Glauber + hydro hybrid) to generate final-state particle spectra and flow vectors. The key analysis will involve direct comparison of calculated v_n{2}, v_n[2], and normalized cumulants like ncε{2,4} and ncε{2,6} with experimental data, using Bayesian inference to constrain deformation parameters and transport coefficients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, structure-informed interpretation of O+O data, establishing a quantitative baseline for nuclear geometry. It will significantly advance the field's ability to distinguish between initial-state and final-state dynamics in small systems, informing the debate on QGP formation thresholds. The constrained deformation parameter for 16O will also serve as valuable input for nuclear structure physics.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have modeled O+O collisions, this proposal uniquely focuses on a direct, quantitative confrontation with the first experimental data using a framework specifically refined for deformation-sensitive flow fluctuations, moving beyond generic Glauber calculations to perform a precision extraction of structure information.

proposals.title: Quantum Monte Carlo Sampling of Correlated Nucleon Configurations for Initial-State Geometry in High-Energy Nuclear Collisions
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the interpretation of collective flow signals in small and large collision systems is fundamentally limited by uncertainties in the initial-state geometry, which originates from the complex, correlated structure of colliding nuclei. Current models often use simplistic, uncorrelated nucleon sampling or pre-defined Woods-Saxon densities, failing to capture realistic short-range correlations and deformation-driven fluctuations. A more rigorous, structure-based sampling method is needed to disentangle final-state flow observables from genuine initial-state nuclear structure effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using ab initio Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) wavefunctions to sample nucleon configurations will provide a more realistic and constrained description of the initial-state geometry, significantly reducing model-dependent uncertainties in the extraction of nuclear deformation parameters and the characterization of sub-nucleonic fluctuations from flow measurements.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Variational Monte Carlo (VMC) and Auxiliary Field Diffusion Monte Carlo (AFDMC) frameworks, which use realistic nuclear forces (e.g., Argonne v18, chiral EFT potentials), to generate ensembles of correlated nucleon positions for key collision isotopes (e.g., 238U, 96Zr, 96Ru). These 3D configurations will serve as direct input to the Trento or IP-Glasma initial-state models to compute energy density profiles. The resulting event-by-event geometry fluctuations and eccentricity coefficients (εn) will be propagated through viscous hydrodynamics to predict flow harmonics ⟨vn⟩ and their fluctuations, which will be compared to RHIC and LHC data.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, ab initio link between nuclear structure theory and heavy-ion collision phenomenology. It will provide a quantitative, first-principles-based uncertainty estimate for initial-state models and deliver new, stringent constraints on nuclear deformation parameters (quadrupole, octupole) by isolating their unique imprint on flow observables from other fluctuation sources.
proposals.novelty: While QMC is a standard tool in low-energy nuclear structure, its application to sample initial conditions for high-energy collisions is novel. This approach moves beyond parameterized nucleon distributions by explicitly incorporating nucleon-nucleon correlations and many-body wavefunction effects into the collision geometry.

proposals.title: Probing alpha-cluster survival and geometry in low-energy $^{16}$O+$^{16}$O collisions via anisotropic flow fluctuations
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies of collective flow in high-energy collisions have successfully probed nuclear deformation. However, the transition to low energies (e.g., < 100 MeV/nucleon) is poorly explored, where the collision dynamics may preserve pre-existing nuclear cluster structures like the alpha-cluster configuration in $^{16}$O. This offers a unique opportunity to study the interplay between cluster survival, initial geometry, and final-state anisotropic flow in a regime dominated by mean-field potentials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the survival of alpha-cluster structures in low-energy $^{16}$O+$^{16}$O collisions will imprint a distinct, event-by-event fluctuating geometry, leading to measurable non-Gaussian fluctuations in elliptic flow ($v_2$) that differ significantly from predictions of standard smooth-nucleus models.
proposals.method: We will employ the AMPT (A Multi-Phase Transport) model with a modified initial condition to explicitly include alpha-cluster configurations for $^{16}$O. Event-by-event anisotropic flow coefficients $v_n$ and their correlations will be calculated using the Q-cumulant method, $\langle v_n^2 \rangle$ and $\langle v_n^4 \rangle$, to extract flow fluctuations and standardized skewness. This will be compared to results from smooth Woods-Saxon densities and state-of-the-art energy density functional theory (DFT) calculations for oxygen. Systematic simulations across a beam energy scan from 20 to 100 MeV/nucleon will map the transition from cluster-driven to bulk-driven flow.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel probe of nuclear clustering in the low-energy, non-perturbative QCD regime. It will provide quantitative constraints on the initial geometric fluctuations in small systems at energies relevant for nuclear astrophysics and bridge the methodology of high-energy flow analysis with low-energy nuclear reaction theory.
proposals.novelty: Applying the sophisticated flow fluctuation framework, previously used for high-energy deformation studies, to the low-energy domain where cluster survival is critical is a novel synthesis. It directly connects sub-nucleonic structure probes across vastly different collision energies.

proposals.title: Constraining Neutron Skin and Nuclear Deformation via the Nn/Np Double Ratio in Isobaric Collisions
proposals.motivation: High-energy isobaric collisions, such as $^{96}_{44}$Ru+$^{96}_{44}$Ru versus $^{96}_{40}$Zr+$^{96}_{40}$Zr, provide a unique opportunity to isolate nuclear structure effects by canceling many systematic uncertainties. Pre-2026 flow measurements in these systems have revealed subtle differences attributed to nuclear deformation and neutron skin, but direct probes of the initial nucleon composition remain challenging due to significant and correlated proton detection efficiency uncertainties.
proposals.hypothesis: The double ratio of neutron multiplicity to proton multiplicity, (Nn/Np)_{RuRu} / (Nn/Np)_{ZrZr}, measured in ultra-central isobaric collisions, is sensitive to the difference in neutron skin thickness and nuclear deformation between the isobars, and is largely independent of detector efficiency effects.
proposals.method: We will apply the participant-plane fluctuation framework developed for flow to the initial-state nucleon composition. Using a Glauber-based model with explicit nucleon degrees of freedom and realistic Woods-Saxon densities for Ru and Zr, we will simulate millions of events to calculate the event-by-event Nn and Np from participating nucleons. The analysis will compute the mean and fluctuations (e.g., $\langle (Nn/Np)^2 \rangle - \langle Nn/Np \rangle^2$) of the Nn/Np ratio for each isobar. The key observable is the double ratio of these quantities between Ru+Ru and Zr+Zr collisions, which cancels common efficiency factors. We will systematically vary the neutron skin and quadrupole/octupole deformation parameters in the nuclear density profiles to map their influence on the double ratio.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, efficiency-robust observable to quantify differences in the initial neutron-proton spatial distributions in isobaric systems. It will deliver quantitative constraints on the neutron skin thickness of $^{96}$Ru and $^{96}$Zr, complementing flow-based studies. The method establishes a new link between final-state hadronic observables and initial-state nuclear structure parameters.
proposals.novelty: While flow fluctuations are established probes of deformation, applying a similar participant-plane fluctuation analysis to the Nn/Np ratio itself is a new direction. The explicit use of the double ratio in isobaric collisions to cancel proton efficiency—a major experimental hurdle—is a novel and practical solution not previously implemented in structure studies.

proposals.title: Probing the Neutron Skin and Nuclear Deformation via the Nn/Np Double Ratio in Collisions of Isotropic Tin Isotopes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the initial-state geometry of colliding nuclei is a critical, yet entangled, input for extracting transport properties of the quark-gluon plasma (QGP) from collective flow. Systematic studies with isotopic chains, like $^{112}$Sn-$^{124}$Sn, offer a unique opportunity to isolate the role of neutron skin thickness and nuclear deformation on final-state observables, as the proton number (and thus charge distribution) is held constant while the neutron density profile changes. This provides a cleaner probe of initial-state nucleonic structure than comparing different elements.
proposals.hypothesis: The double ratio of neutron-to-proton multiplicity (Nn/Np) in symmetric collisions of neutron-rich vs. neutron-poor isotopes is sensitive to the neutron skin and, through its correlation with anisotropic flow coefficients $v_n$ and their fluctuations $\langle v_n^2 \rangle - \langle v_n \rangle^2$, can disentangle these structural effects from QGP response.
proposals.method: We will perform a combined analysis using state-of-the-art pre-2026 tools. Initial conditions for $^{112,116,120,124}$Sn+Sn collisions will be generated using the TRENTo model with parametric nucleon distributions, varying the neutron skin thickness according to density functional theory predictions. These will be propagated through viscous hydrodynamics (e.g., MUSIC) and a hadronic cascade. The primary observable is the event-by-event Nn/Np ratio from spectator nucleons or late-stage emission, correlated with participant eccentricities $\varepsilon_n$. We will quantify the sensitivity by calculating the Pearson coefficients $\rho(\text{Nn/Np}, \varepsilon_n)$ and the scaling of $v_n\{\Psi_m\}$ with Nn/Np across the isotopic chain.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, structure-sensitive double-ratio observable for relativistic heavy-ion collisions, directly linking isotopic differences to initial-state geometry. It will provide quantitative constraints on the neutron skin thickness of stable nuclei, complementing low-energy experiments, and clarify the impact of nuclear structure uncertainties on precision extraction of the QGP shear viscosity.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies probed deformation using flow in different elements (e.g., U+U vs. Au+Au), this proposal systematically applies the Nn/Np ratio—a direct measure of neutron density—within an isotopic chain to isolate the neutron skin's effect, a more controlled test of subnucleonic structure.

proposals.title: Disentangling Deformation and Electromagnetic Excitation Effects on Collective Flow in High-Energy Nuclear Collisions
proposals.motivation: Recent pre-2026 analyses of flow observables, particularly in small systems and collisions involving deformed nuclei like $^{238}$U, have highlighted the significant influence of nuclear structure. However, a concurrent subdominant effect in ultra-peripheral and peripheral collisions is electromagnetic (EM) excitation, which can alter the initial state of the colliding nuclei. A systematic, quantitative framework to isolate and compare these two initial-state effects is lacking, limiting the precision of using flow as a clean probe of nuclear structure.
proposals.hypothesis: The subleading contributions to flow fluctuations and anisotropic flow coefficients in specific centrality classes can be decomposed into distinct, quantifiable components arising from static nuclear deformation and dynamic EM excitation, with the latter's signature being enhanced in asymmetric collision systems.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-step analysis using data from RHIC and the LHC. First, we will use the established $\langle \mathrm{cos}(n(\Psi_n - \Psi_m))\rangle$ correlators and symmetric cumulants to map flow fluctuations across centrality. We will then implement a Bayesian inference framework, feeding in initial conditions from state-of-the-art Monte Carlo Glauber models (e.g., TRENTo) explicitly extended to include quadrupole and octupole deformations. Concurrently, we will simulate EM dissociation using pre-2026 codes like STARlight to generate event-by-event distributions of neutron emission spectators. The key step is a comparative analysis of symmetric (e.g., U+U) and asymmetric (e.g., U+Au, p+U) systems to separate the deformation-driven signal from the EM excitation background.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative bounds on the EM excitation background in flow-based measurements of nuclear deformation. It will deliver a calibrated, public framework for future precision studies of exotic nuclear shapes in collision experiments and refine the understanding of initial-state fluctuations by isolating a specific dynamical source.
proposals.novelty: While deformation and EM effects have been studied separately, this proposal innovates by developing a joint quantification framework and leveraging asymmetric collision systems as a critical tool to disentangle their overlapping signatures on final-state flow observables.

proposals.title: Energy Dependence of Nuclear Deformation Signatures in Collective Flow: A Systematic Study from RHIC to the LHC
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies have established that collective flow observables in high-energy nuclear collisions are sensitive to the initial-state nuclear structure, including quadrupole and octupole deformations. However, this sensitivity has been quantified primarily at a single collision energy per facility (e.g., top RHIC or LHC energies). The evolution of this sensitivity with collision energy is unknown, yet it is crucial for disentangling initial-state structure effects from the dynamical response of the created quark-gluon plasma, which itself is energy-dependent.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sensitivity of anisotropic flow coefficients and their fluctuations to nuclear deformation parameters exhibits a non-monotonic energy dependence, driven by the competing effects of initial spatial anisotropy preservation and the changing transport properties of the QGP.
proposals.method: We will perform a systematic analysis using the AMPT model and hydrodynamic simulations with initial conditions from the TRENTo model, incorporating Woods-Saxon densities with deformation parameters. We will generate events for collisions of deformed nuclei (e.g., 238U, 96Zr) and spherical references across a beam energy range of √s_NN = 20 GeV to 5.02 TeV. The primary observables will be the differential flow vector correlations, ⟨v_n^2⟩, and especially the normalized symmetric cumulants, NSC(n,m), and flow fluctuations, which are established pre-2026 probes of deformation. We will quantify the sensitivity via a statistical metric (e.g., Pearson coefficient) between the observable and the input deformation parameter across energies.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive map of how initial nuclear structure imprints evolve across the QCD phase diagram. It will provide essential guidance for future deformation measurements at facilities like the RHIC BES II and fixed-target LHC programs, and constrain energy-dependent models of initial-state geometry and medium response.
proposals.novelty: While individual studies exist at fixed energies, a controlled, energy-scanning analysis focused explicitly on the evolution of deformation-sensitive observables is novel. It moves beyond a static picture to a dynamical one, testing how structure information is transmitted through a varying QGP medium.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of Neutron Skin Thickness on Initial-State Geometry and Final-State Flow Observables in High-Energy Nuclear Collisions
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the precise characterization of the initial geometry in heavy-ion collisions is crucial for extracting transport properties of the quark-gluon plasma (QGP). While nuclear deformation is known to affect initial-state eccentricities and flow harmonics, the role of the neutron skin—a difference in the radial distributions of neutrons and protons—remains poorly quantified. This effect is a key nuclear structure observable but its systematic influence on collision geometry and flow fluctuations is not yet disentangled from other structure effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neutron skin thickness, by modifying the nucleon density profiles in colliding nuclei, introduces a distinct, mass-number-dependent scaling in the initial-state eccentricity fluctuations, which propagates linearly to the final-state anisotropic flow coefficients $v_n$ and their correlations.
proposals.method: We will employ a Bayesian framework, using Trento initial condition models extended with distinct neutron and proton Woods-Saxon distributions to generate event-by-event initial entropy density profiles. The neutron skin thickness ($\Delta R_{np}$) will be systematically varied for isobaric and isotopic collision systems (e.g., $^{96}$Ru/$^{96}$Zr, $^{208}$Pb, $^{238}$U). These initial conditions will be propagated through viscous hydrodynamics (e.g., MUSIC) and hadronic transport. We will compute flow observables, including $\langle v_n \rangle$, $v_n$ fluctuations, and symmetric cumulants $SC(m,n)$, and perform a principal component analysis to isolate the specific scaling patterns induced by $\Delta R_{np}$.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic quantification of the neutron skin's impact on QGP flow observables, establishing a new connection between low-energy nuclear structure and high-energy collision dynamics. It will deliver crucial constraints for initial-state models and inform the interpretation of isobar collision data, potentially offering a novel, high-energy probe of the neutron skin thickness itself.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Jia et al. focused on quadrupole and octupole deformation, this proposal isolates and quantifies the effect of the neutron skin—a distinct, monopole structure feature—on flow fluctuations, moving beyond a simple 'hard-sphere' or uniform nucleon density assumption in initial condition models.

proposals.title: Disentangling Initial-State Geometry from Final-State Response: A Dual-System Analysis of Flow Fluctuations in p-Pb and Pb-Pb Collisions at the LHC
proposals.motivation: In high-energy nuclear collisions, anisotropic flow coefficients and their event-by-event fluctuations are powerful probes of the initial-state geometry and the properties of the quark-gluon plasma (QGP). However, interpreting flow measurements in heavy-ion collisions like Pb-Pb is complicated by the entanglement of the initial nuclear deformation (structure) with the non-linear hydrodynamic response of the QGP. A clean baseline where nuclear shape effects are negligible is needed to isolate the final-state hydrodynamic response function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the event-by-event flow magnitude and correlation fluctuations in small, asymmetric p-Pb collisions, where initial geometry is dominated by nucleon-scale fluctuations rather than global nuclear deformation, provide a control dataset that characterizes the hydrodynamic response. This response function can then be used to isolate the specific contributions of quadrupole and octupole nuclear deformations to flow observables in symmetric Pb-Pb collisions.
proposals.method: We will perform a coupled analysis of Pb-Pb and p-Pb data at sqrt(s_NN)=5.02 TeV from the ALICE experiment. Using standard two-particle cumulant and scalar product methods, we will measure the probability distributions p(v_n) and symmetric cumulants SC(n,m) for n=2,3,4 in both systems. A key step is to use the p-Pb measurements, along with state-of-the-art TRENTo or IP-Glasma initial-state models tuned to p-Pb data, to constrain the hydrodynamic response coefficients and their fluctuations via Bayesian inference. This calibrated response will then be applied to Pb-Pb initial conditions that explicitly include U-238 quadrupole (β2) and octupole (β3) deformations, allowing us to extract β2 and β3 by comparing calculated and measured Pb-Pb flow fluctuations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven framework for separating initial-state nuclear structure from final-state physics in heavy-ion collisions. It will yield a more robust extraction of the Uranium-238 deformation parameters from high-energy collision data and establish a methodology for using small systems as a control for precision QGP studies.
proposals.novelty: While p-Pb collisions are often studied as small systems, their systematic use as a hydrodynamic response control to deconvolve nuclear structure effects in A-A collisions is a non-obvious and untapped strategy. This dual-system approach moves beyond direct A-A comparisons to explicitly separate the source (structure) from the transport (response).

proposals.title: Spectator Final-State Interactions as a Probe of Early-Time Dynamics and Nuclear Deformation in High-Energy Collisions
proposals.motivation: In high-energy nuclear collisions, the initial geometry, shaped by nuclear structure, is a critical input for modeling collective flow. Current models treat spectator nucleons as inert fragments that do not interact after the initial overlap, yet pre-2026 data on spectator deflection and fragmentation suggests complex final-state dynamics. A quantitative model of these interactions is needed to determine if they bias the extraction of initial-state geometry, particularly for deformation-sensitive observables like flow fluctuations and symmetric-asymmetric comparisons.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that final-state interactions between receding spectators and the quark-gluon plasma or fireball remnants introduce measurable, geometry-dependent modifications to spectator kinematics, which in turn affect the inferred participant plane and flow coefficients.
proposals.method: We will extend the TRENTo initial condition model coupled to a hadronic afterburner (e.g., SMASH or UrQMD) to include a time-dependent mean-field potential between spectators and the dense central region. The potential strength will be parameterized based on pre-2026 constraints from spectator neutron multiplicity and deflection data. We will implement a Monte Carlo Glauber framework where spectator nucleons, post-overlap, are propagated under this potential, and their modified trajectories will be used to recalculate the participant plane anisotropy vectors ($\langle \epsilon_n \rangle$) and flow coefficients ($v_n$). The model will be validated against Au+Au and U+U data from RHIC.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic assessment of spectator final-state effects on flow observables, quantifying potential biases in deformation measurements. It will deliver a publicly available module for initial-state models that improves the fidelity of the participant-spectator separation, crucial for interpreting small-system and isobar collision data.
proposals.novelty: While spectator dynamics are often ignored in flow analyses, this proposal explicitly models their post-overlap interactions as a novel source of systematic uncertainty and a potential independent probe of the early-time pressure gradient.

proposals.title: Constraining the Symmetry Energy Slope Parameter L via the Free Neutron-to-Proton Ratio in High-Energy Nuclear Collisions
proposals.motivation: The density dependence of the nuclear symmetry energy, characterized by its slope parameter L, is a critical but poorly constrained component of the nuclear equation of state (EoS). It profoundly impacts neutron star structure, heavy-ion collision dynamics, and the neutron skin thickness of nuclei. Pre-2026, extracting L from heavy-ion collisions has been challenging due to model dependencies and the conflation of initial-state nuclear structure effects with the EoS-driven dynamical evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: The measured mid-rapidity free neutron-to-proton ratio (n/p) in high-energy symmetric and asymmetric nuclear collisions is a sensitive and direct probe of the symmetry energy's density dependence, allowing for a model-constrained extraction of the slope parameter L, independent of initial-state deformation effects.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-step, multi-model analysis. First, we will use the AMPT and/or 3D-Glauber models to generate initial conditions for collisions of isobaric and asymmetric systems (e.g., Ru+Ru, Zr+Zr, Ru+Zr). These will be propagated through a relativistic transport model (e.g., UrQMD) with a tunable symmetry energy potential term. We will systematically vary the slope parameter L in the model's EoS and calculate the resulting n/p ratio. A Bayesian analysis framework will then be used to compare model calculations with existing experimental data on n/p ratios and flow observables, constraining the posterior distribution for L and isolating its effect from initial-state structure fluctuations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, collision-driven constraint on the symmetry energy slope L, complementary to astrophysical and low-energy nuclear structure measurements. It will establish a direct link between a clean experimental observable (free n/p ratio) and a fundamental property of dense matter, reducing systematic uncertainties from initial-state modeling by leveraging the researcher's expertise in disentangling structure and EoS effects.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies used collective flow or pion yields to probe the symmetry energy, this proposal directly utilizes the free nucleon ratio, a more fundamental isospin observable, and explicitly decouples the influence of nuclear octupole/quadrupole deformation—a key pre-2026 advancement—from the genuine EoS signal using symmetric-asymmetric collision comparisons.

proposals.title: Probing Neutron Skin via Centrality Definition Sensitivity: A Systematic Study of Free Neutron Ratio in Ultracentral U+U and Pb+Pb Collisions
proposals.motivation: In ultracentral heavy-ion collisions, the initial geometry is dominated by the nuclear structure of the colliding ions. The free neutron ratio, often approximated by the ratio of neutron to proton multiplicity, is a sensitive probe of the neutron skin thickness. However, extracting this signal is complicated by the experimental definition of centrality, which relies on particle multiplicity and is inherently model-dependent. Pre-2026 studies have shown that flow observables are sensitive to nuclear deformation, but the systematic uncertainty from centrality selection on structure-sensitive ratios remains unquantified.
proposals.hypothesis: The measured free neutron ratio in ultracentral collisions exhibits a significant and quantifiable sensitivity to the precise algorithmic definition of centrality, and this sensitivity pattern differs between collision systems (e.g., U+U vs. Pb+Pb) due to their distinct nuclear structure profiles.
proposals.method: We will employ a hybrid modeling approach using state-of-the-art Monte Carlo event generators (e.g., TRENTo, AMPT) with realistic nuclear density distributions for $^{238}$U and $^{208}$Pb, incorporating Woods-Saxon parameters and octupole/quadrupole deformations. For each event, we will compute the true participant nucleon count ($N_{\mathrm{part}}$) and the free neutron number. We will then apply multiple centrality definition algorithms (e.g., charged particle multiplicity in different pseudorapidity ranges, energy deposition in zero-degree calorimeters) to the simulated final-state particles. The correlation between the defined centrality percentile and the underlying $N_{\mathrm{part}}$ and neutron ratio will be analyzed statistically, quantifying the bias and fluctuations ($\langle...\rangle$ and event-by-event fluctuations) introduced by each definition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework to quantify and correct for centrality-definition biases in nuclear structure observables extracted from ultracentral collisions. It will deliver concrete recommendations for optimal centrality selection to minimize systematic errors in neutron skin measurements. The results will directly impact the interpretation of data from the RHIC and LHC beam energy scan programs regarding the equation of state of asymmetric nuclear matter.
proposals.novelty: While centrality dependence of flow is well-studied, the systematic bias of centrality definition on a direct nucleonic observable like the free neutron ratio is unexplored. This study uniquely connects experimental methodology (centrality algorithms) to a fundamental nuclear structure property (neutron skin), moving beyond averaged geometry to fluctuation-driven biases.

proposals.title: Bayesian Inference of Nuclear Deformation Parameters from Collective Flow Observables in High-Energy Collisions
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work has established that final-state anisotropic flow, especially its fluctuations, is sensitive to the initial-state geometry of colliding nuclei, which is governed by nuclear structure parameters like quadrupole (β2) and octupole (β3) deformation. Current analyses often assume a deformation and compute the resulting flow. A robust, quantitative inverse method to systematically extract these fundamental structure parameters from collision data is a critical unmet need, bridging nuclear physics and relativistic heavy-ion phenomenology.
proposals.hypothesis: That a Bayesian inference framework, treating the Trento or AMPT initial-condition model as a forward simulator, can reliably extract posterior probability distributions for nuclear deformation parameters from experimental flow cumulants, such as ⟨v_n⟩ and v_n{2}, measured in collisions of deformed nuclei.
proposals.method: We will employ a Bayesian workflow using the Trento model with parameterized nuclear densities (Woods-Saxon with deformation) as the forward model to generate initial-state entropy profiles. These will be propagated through a computationally efficient hydrodynamic emulator (e.g., a trained neural network) to predict flow observables. Using experimental data for systems like 238U+238U and 96Ru+96Ru, we will perform Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to infer the posterior distributions for β2 and β3, rigorously incorporating systematic uncertainties from model parameters (e.g., nucleon width, fluctuation scaling).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, uncertainty-quantified extraction of nuclear deformation parameters from high-energy collision data, establishing a new quantitative tool for nuclear structure. It will clarify the interplay between collective flow and initial geometry, offering a novel constraint complementary to low-energy experiments and advancing the use of heavy-ion collisions as a precision probe of the nucleus.
proposals.novelty: While forward calculations linking deformation to flow exist, this proposal inverts the problem using a rigorous Bayesian framework, moving from qualitative sensitivity studies to quantitative parameter extraction with full uncertainty quantification, a novel application in this domain.

ideas.title: Energy-Dependent Extraction of Nuclear Deformation from Ratio Observables
ideas.description: This research direction systematically applies the method of inverting deformation parameters from ratio observables (like Nch vs. centrality) across a wide range of collision energies, from top RHIC/LHC down to lower energies at RHIC, FAIR, and NICA. By treating β2 and β3 as energy-dependent unknowns to be extracted at each beam energy, the study moves beyond a single snapshot of nuclear shape. This enables the investigation of how the apparent deformation signal, as seen through the lens of collective flow and particle production, changes with the initial energy density and system lifetime. The synergy provides a unique tool to disentangle genuine initial-state nuclear structure from the energy-dependent modifications imposed by the dynamical evolution of the created medium.

ideas.title: Computer Vision-Guided, Event-by-Event Medium Evolution for Isobar Collisions
ideas.description: This research direction uses advanced computer vision techniques (e.g., convolutional neural networks) to extract a high-dimensional feature set characterizing the initial-state geometry from event-by-event nucleon configurations in isobar collisions. These features are then used as conditional inputs to a flexible hydrodynamic model that does not assume identical medium properties for Ru+Ru and Zr+Zr, allowing evolution parameters like viscosity or equation of state to vary. By correlating final-state observables with the vision-extracted initial features through this variable evolution model, the approach can disentangle and quantify differences in the medium evolution that are not reducible to initial geometry alone. This provides a powerful, model-constrained method to directly test for and identify the microscopic origins of observed collective flow differences between the isobar systems.

ideas.title: Adapting Multi-Physics Calibration from Climate Modeling to Heavy-Ion Collisions
ideas.description: This idea challenges the common assumption that the quark-gluon plasma (QGP) medium, once formed, evolves identically in isobaric collisions like Ru+Ru and Zr+Zr, aside from differences in initial geometry. Drawing an analogy from climate science, where different components of the Earth system have distinct, calibrated physics, we propose a solution. In climate modeling, parameters for atmospheric physics, ocean dynamics, and ice sheets are often tuned independently using diverse data sources within a coupled model framework. Adapted to heavy-ion physics, this means developing a calibration procedure for a dynamical model (e.g., viscous hydrodynamics) where key medium properties, such as transport coefficients or equation-of-state parameters, are allowed to take different values for Ru+Ru and Zr+Zr systems. The model would be simultaneously constrained by the full suite of experimental measurements from both collision types, testing whether the data necessitate distinct evolution parameters and thereby probing subtle nuclear structure effects on QGP dynamics beyond initial-state geometry.

ideas.title: Calibrating the Phase Transition 'Lens' with Low-Energy Ratio Observables
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from planetary science, where the ratio of seismic wave velocities (Vp/Vs) is used as a calibrated probe to map phase transitions and composition changes within a planet's interior. The analogous problem in heavy-ion physics is using a simple, robust observable to probe the equation of state and phase structure of QCD matter. The solution is to treat the ratio of charged particle multiplicity (Nch) to collision centrality not just as a scaling variable, but as a sensitive 'seismic ratio' for the collision fireball. The adapted research idea is to perform a systematic, high-precision measurement of this Nch/centrality ratio observable across a continuous spectrum of lower collision energies at facilities like RHIC (low-energy scans) and FAIR/NICA. By tracing how this ratio evolves with the 'depth' (i.e., collision energy √s_NN, which controls net baryon density and temperature), one can search for distinctive changes in slope or non-monotonic features. Such signatures would act as the 'seismic discontinuities' revealing the softening of the equation of state or boundaries between different phases of nuclear matter, providing a calibrated, model-independent lens on the QCD phase diagram at high baryon density.

ideas.title: Causal Inference for Event Activity Selection in Collisions of Large Deformed Nuclei
ideas.description: This research direction applies causal inference methods, such as directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) and potential outcomes frameworks, to the complex initial conditions of collisions between large deformed nuclei like Uranium-238 or hypothetical superheavy isotopes. The goal is to rigorously define and estimate the causal effect of choosing specific event activity variables (e.g., participant nucleon count, transverse energy) in these systems, where extreme deformation and size introduce strong geometric confounding. By scaling the causal analysis to these larger systems, the method can systematically separate the true causal influence of initial geometric shape from other correlated initial-state effects and non-linear medium evolution, providing a principled way to validate hydrodynamic and initial-state models against experimental data from facilities like RHIC and the LHC.

ideas.title: Isolating QGP medium properties using isobar subtraction inspired by control experiments in pharmacology
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by using analogical reasoning from pharmacology. The core challenge is to isolate the 'pure' signal of Quark-Gluon Plasma (QGP) medium properties from confounding 'background' effects arising from initial nuclear structure differences. In pharmacology, the gold standard for isolating a drug's effect is the controlled trial with a placebo group, where the difference between treatment and control groups reveals the drug's action. Adapted to heavy-ion physics, this translates to designing controlled collision 'experiments' using carefully selected pairs of nuclear systems. For instance, collisions of isobaric nuclei (like ⁹⁶Ru+⁹⁶Ru and ⁹⁶Zr+⁹⁶Zr) have nearly identical mass but different shapes and neutron skin thicknesses. By measuring an observable (e.g., flow harmonics, particle yields, or correlation functions) in both systems and constructing a ratio or a subtracted difference, the common QGP response cancels out. The residual is a direct measure of the nuclear structure effect. To achieve the original goal, one then inverts the logic: identify observables and system pairs where theoretical models predict these nuclear structure residuals to be negligible. Any significant difference observed in the experimental comparison between the two systems can then be attributed more confidently to differences in the created QGP's properties (such as its specific shear viscosity, equation of state, or opacity), thereby isolating the medium's intrinsic behavior.

ideas.title: Bayesian Disentanglement of Initial-State Structure and Dynamical Response using a Full Suite of Flow Observables
ideas.description: This study develops a comprehensive Bayesian analysis framework to quantitatively separate the imprints of nuclear skin and deformation from the non-linear hydrodynamic and hadronic response in flow harmonics. By generating a massive prior from an ensemble of initial-condition and dynamical models with controlled parameters, the framework performs a global fit to high-precision measurements of v2, v3, v4, and their correlations across collision systems (e.g., Cu+Cu, Au+Au, U+U, Xe+Xe) and centralities. The simultaneous constraint from the full set of observables breaks key degeneracies, allowing for a statistically rigorous extraction of initial-state structure parameters and their uncertainties, moving beyond a simple qualitative investigation of separation.

ideas.title: Systematic Sensitivity Analysis of Nuclear Structure Parameters via a Design-of-Experiments (DOE) Framework in AMPT
ideas.description: This research adapts the Design of Experiments (DOE) methodology, commonly used in engineering and materials science for multi-parameter sensitivity analysis, to the study of nuclear structure in heavy-ion collisions. The analogous solution involves using structured factorial designs to efficiently map how multiple input factors combine to influence an output. Adapted to the original seed idea, this means running the AMPT model not with a simple one-at-a-time parameter variation, but with a full or fractional factorial design where the parameters R0, a0, β2, and β3 are varied together across a defined grid. The output observables (ΔR, particle ratios) from this simulation matrix are then analyzed using analysis of variance (ANOVA) and regression techniques. This will quantitatively decompose the total variance in the observables into contributions from each individual parameter and from their two-, three-, and four-way interactions, providing a complete, systematic map of how nuclear structure parameters individually and collectively influence collision outcomes.

ideas.title: Coordinated Multi-Model Transport Test with Isolated Dynamics for Nuclear Parameter Sensitivity
ideas.description: This study establishes a controlled framework to validate findings on nuclear parameter dominance by isolating the transport dynamics of different models. A protocol is created to run AMPT, UrQMD, and PHSD using identical initial conditions from a common source and a shared afterburner, ensuring differences originate from their core collision evolution mechanisms. A defined suite of sensitive observables is calculated, and a systematic variation of the key nuclear parameters is performed within each model's setup. The resulting multi-model sensitivity matrices are then compared to distinguish robust physical insights from model-specific artifacts.

ideas.title: Simultaneous Bayesian extraction of initial geometry and QGP properties via multi-observable constraint
ideas.description: This approach reframes the inverse problem as a joint inference challenge, directly addressing the circularity of the original idea. A comprehensive model chain, from initial-state entropy deposition through viscous hydrodynamic evolution to hadronic transport, is embedded within a Bayesian statistical framework. A wide array of soft hadron and flow observables from heavy-ion collisions at different centralities and collision energies are used to simultaneously constrain the posterior probability distributions for parameters describing the initial nuclear geometry and the QGP medium's transport properties. The result is a self-consistent, uncertainty-quantified extraction where constraints on geometry and medium properties inform and refine each other, producing a more robust and physically complete picture of the collision system.

ideas.title: A Criterion-Driven Framework for Extending the Nch-Ratio Method to Validated Observables
ideas.description: This research develops a principled framework for extending the charged-particle multiplicity (Nch) ratio method beyond its established uses. It first establishes strict selection criteria, based on scaling behavior and physical origin, to identify which new observables are theoretically suitable for the technique. Candidate observables, such as those related to jet quenching, strangeness production, or charge-dependent correlations, are then rigorously tested and validated in simulated reference systems like proton-proton collisions. Only observables passing this validation are applied to heavy-ion data, ensuring the resulting ratios provide robust insights into initial-state vs. final-state effects.

ideas.title: Leveraging Parameter Sensitivity Ratios as a Model Discriminant
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by using analogical reasoning from climate model evaluation. In climate science, models are tested not just on a final output like global temperature, but on the pattern of sensitivities to underlying physical feedbacks. Similarly, in heavy-ion collisions, we propose using the ratios of sensitivities (∂(ΔR)/∂p_i) / (∂(ΔR)/∂p_j) for different nuclear parameters (p_i, p_j) as a stringent test for initial-state models. A model is considered physically plausible only if it reproduces both the measured ΔR observable and the experimentally inferred ratios of how ΔR changes with variations in fundamental inputs. This method exposes models that achieve the correct final result through erroneous, compensating parameter dependencies, thereby providing a more powerful discriminant than the observable alone.

ideas.title: Coulomb-Mitigated Probes of Deformation in Ultra-Heavy Ion Collisions
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea focuses on systematically overcoming the Coulomb and density barriers to studying deformation in collisions of ultra-heavy nuclei like Uranium. It employs high-fidelity theoretical simulations to pre-select viable collision scenarios where geometric deformation signatures survive, rather than applying a method blindly. The approach then leverages advanced machine learning-based trigger systems and high-precision vertex and flow detectors at facilities like RHIC or the LHC to filter and analyze these specific events, transforming the study from a brute-force application into a targeted search for deformation-driven phenomena in an extreme regime.

ideas.title: Apply Bayesian inference from epidemiology to nuclear collision data
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from epidemiology, where Bayesian inference is used to infer disease model parameters from noisy, real-world observational data. The analogous problem in heavy-ion collision physics is inferring fundamental nuclear parameters (e.g., the equation of state, shear viscosity) from complex, multi-faceted collision data. By treating collision observables as the epidemiological 'case data' and the nuclear collision simulation as the 'transmission model', we can employ established Bayesian MCMC frameworks to sample the posterior probability distribution of the nuclear parameters. This provides a statistically rigorous method to quantify the constraints and uncertainties placed on nuclear theory by experimental data, directly transferring the probabilistic reasoning used to understand disease spread to understanding the physics of quark-gluon plasma.

ideas.title: Correlation Matrix of Conserved Charge Ratios for Model Disentanglement
ideas.description: This approach constructs a matrix of correlated ratios of conserved charge observables (e.g., baryon number, strangeness) across collision systems and energies. The system leverages the fact that different model components (initial-state geometry, pre-equilibrium dynamics, hydrodynamic transport, hadronic cascade) imprint distinct, predictable correlation patterns on this matrix. By performing a global Bayesian analysis that requires a single set of parameters to reproduce the entire correlation structure, the method can identify and reject models where tuning to one ratio fatally disrupts the agreement with others, thereby rigorously testing the internal consistency of the model framework itself.

ideas.title: Applying the Nch Ratio as a System-Response Calibrator for Hard Probes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the methodology of scaling relation calibration from astrophysics to heavy-ion physics. In cosmology, indirect proxies like the Sunyaev-Zel'dovich effect are calibrated against direct measurements to enable large-scale surveys. Similarly, the goal is to extend the Nch-based ratio analysis beyond multiplicity, flow, and CME-sensitive observables by establishing Nch as a foundational event-scale variable that encodes global system properties. The adapted research plan is to identify candidate 'hard probe' observables (e.g., jet quenching parameters, initial state eccentricities, or particle yield ratios for rare species) and, for a well-defined event ensemble, empirically determine their functional relationship with Nch. This calibrated 'Nch scaling relation' would then allow the application of the ratio method: by comparing Nch distributions between two collision systems or centrality selections, one can predict the corresponding scaling and thus the expected change in the target observable, providing a powerful constraint for theory and a new tool for analyzing statistically challenging event classes.

ideas.title: Multi-Model Bayesian Inference for Nuclear Parameters with Explicit Model Discrepancy
ideas.description: This approach employs a Bayesian hierarchical framework to calibrate nuclear structure parameters against heavy-ion collision data while rigorously accounting for uncertainty in the theoretical forward models. The system performs concurrent calibration on an ensemble of state-of-the-art transport models (e.g., SMASH, AMPT, UrQMD, JAM) within a unified inference process. It incorporates explicit, learnable model discrepancy terms for each simulator to capture structural errors, and uses Bayesian Model Averaging to produce robust parameter posteriors that are marginalized over model choice. This yields constraints that are not only data-informed but also explicitly quantify the uncertainty originating from our imperfect knowledge of the correct underlying physical model.

ideas.title: Apply Topological Data Analysis to Initial Energy Distributions via Cosmic Web Mapping Techniques
ideas.description: This idea adapts the application of topological data analysis (TDA), specifically persistent homology, from cosmology to the study of initial conditions in heavy-ion collisions. In cosmology, TDA is successfully used to characterize the multi-scale topology (clusters, filaments, voids) of the cosmic web's matter density. The analogous solution involves constructing a filtration over the density field and computing persistent homology barcodes/diagrams to quantify these features in a scale-invariant manner. Adapted to heavy-ion physics, we apply the same technique to the initial energy density distributions in the transverse plane, generated by theoretical models. This provides a robust, geometric characterization of the 'shape' of the initial state—capturing the connectivity and void structure of the hot spot network—which can then be correlated with final-state flow observables to probe the sensitivity of hydrodynamic evolution to initial geometry beyond traditional eccentricity coefficients.

ideas.title: ML-Optimized Event Classifiers for Deconvolving Initial-State Nuclear Structure from Final-State Observables
ideas.description: This research fuses the concepts of ML-optimized event classification and the inverse problem of extracting initial conditions. It proposes training machine learning models to identify or construct a novel event activity variable that is maximally sensitive to the initial participant nucleon count (Nch) and minimally contaminated by nonflow and other final-state backgrounds. This optimized classifier then serves as the primary observable for a subsequent inverse mapping, using known nuclear structure parameters (like nuclear density profiles) to predict the full Nch distribution. The synergy creates a self-improving cycle where the classifier's design is explicitly informed by the needs of the inverse problem, leading to more robust extraction of initial-state physics from heavy-ion collision data.

ideas.title: Inverse Tomography: Using QGP Final-State Observables to Reconstruct Initial Nuclear Geometry
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of inverse tomography from fields like geophysics to heavy-ion physics. The core challenge is to invert causality: instead of using the initial nuclear geometry to predict QGP properties, we assume the QGP's medium properties (equation of state, transport coefficients) are sufficiently known from other constraints. We then use the final-state particle emission patterns—analogous to seismic wave recordings—as inputs. By employing a highly-tuned model of the QGP's space-time evolution (the 'forward model'), we formulate and solve a Bayesian inverse problem. The solution provides a probabilistic reconstruction of the initial nuclear geometry parameters (e.g., eccentricities, participant nucleon distributions, granularity), thereby using the QGP as a precision probe of nuclear structure at the moment of collision.

ideas.title: Quantitative Model Discrimination via Parameter Sensitivity Ratios
ideas.description: This research direction applies global sensitivity analysis (Sobol indices) to calculate not just the absolute influence of nuclear parameters on the initial-state observable ΔR, but their precise sensitivity ratios. These ratios, being intensive properties, serve as stringent discriminators between models. A model is validated only if its predicted sensitivity pattern matches the empirical pattern extracted from experimental data, providing a fundamental test of whether the model's encoded QCD physics correctly propagates to final-state correlations. This creates a dual-purpose tool: it rigorously ranks parameter importance for constraint and simultaneously evaluates the physical plausibility of the models themselves.

ideas.title: Disentangling collective and non-collective sources in higher-order flow cumulants via multi-variable event activity correlations
ideas.description: This study systematically investigates ratios of higher-order cumulants of flow harmonics by correlating them with multiple, independent measures of event activity. The core methodology involves measuring these cumulant ratios as functions of different activity variables, such as forward transverse energy and reference subevent multiplicities, which have varying sensitivities to volume fluctuations and hard processes. A subsequent statistical analysis, including covariance studies and multi-parameter fits, is performed to disentangle the genuine collective flow fluctuation signal from contaminating non-flow and volume fluctuation effects. This approach provides a more robust and unambiguous probe of the initial-state geometry and its hydrodynamic response than single-variable methods.

ideas.title: Inverse Problem: Bayesian Nch Reconstruction from Centrality via Seismic Tomography Analogy
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the inverse problem in seismic tomography to the heavy-ion collision context. The core challenge is to develop a mapping that predicts the charged-particle multiplicity (Nch) distribution given a centrality class and known nuclear structure parameters—an inversion of the typical forward calculation. Drawing from geophysics, where surface seismic data is inverted to reveal subsurface structure, we propose a Bayesian inversion framework. Here, the 'subsurface structure' is the initial state of the colliding nuclei, characterized by known parameters. The 'surface data' is the experimental centrality classifier. The method involves constructing a forward model (e.g., an advanced Monte Carlo Glauber+hydrodynamic/hadronization simulator) that generates Nch distributions from nuclear profiles. A Bayesian inference engine then inverts this relationship: given a centrality measurement (a constraint on the total activity), it samples the space of possible initial-state configurations consistent with the known nuclear parameters to reconstruct the full posterior probability distribution for Nch. This provides a principled, uncertainty-quantified mapping from centrality to Nch, effectively solving the inverse problem by treating the collision as a tomographic probe of the nuclear initial condition.

ideas.title: Bias-Corrected Bayesian Synthesis for Nuclear Inference via Tiered Priors and Domain Transfer
ideas.description: This approach develops a robust Bayesian framework to infer nuclear parameters from heavy-ion collision data while cautiously incorporating insights from astrophysics. It first constructs foundational, weakly informative priors directly from theoretical calculations valid for the collision regime to prevent astrophysical assumptions from dominating. A core innovation is a diagnostic and correction module that explicitly quantifies the bias introduced by any cross-domain prior information, using a transfer learning metric. The final inference is therefore driven by the collision data, with astrophysical knowledge acting as a constrained, bias-corrected supplement rather than a dominant prior, yielding more reliable and domain-appropriate nuclear parameters.

ideas.title: Adapting Geospatial Terrain Analysis for Nucleon Configuration Classification
ideas.description: This idea adapts computer vision techniques from geospatial terrain analysis to classify and quantify initial-state geometry in heavy-ion collisions. In geospatial analysis, convolutional neural networks and feature extraction algorithms are used to classify terrain types (e.g., mountains, valleys) and extract quantitative metrics (roughness, elevation profiles) from satellite imagery. Similarly, nucleon configurations from Monte Carlo simulations (e.g., Glauber, T\textsubscript{R}ENTo) can be treated as 2D 'terrain maps' where nucleon positions create a density landscape. By training vision models on these simulated maps, we can automate the identification of geometric features critical to heavy-ion physics—such as eccentricity harmonics, participant nucleon distributions, and initial-state fluctuations—providing a fast, consistent, and potentially more comprehensive method for characterizing initial conditions compared to traditional analytical calculations.

ideas.title: Inverse Problem for Nuclear Deformation via Observables
ideas.description: This idea inverts the standard approach to nuclear deformation parameters (β2, β3). Instead of using them as inputs to calculate observables, they are treated as unknowns to be extracted from a set of ratio observables. The analogy is drawn from geophysical seismic tomography, where the Earth's internal structure is inferred from surface measurements of seismic waves. The analogous solution involves setting up an inverse problem: a forward model (e.g., a collective Hamiltonian or mean-field model) calculates theoretical ratios from trial deformation parameters. An inversion algorithm (e.g., least-squares minimization or Bayesian sampling) then finds the deformation parameter values that provide the best global fit to the entire available set of experimental ratio observables, effectively performing 'nuclear structure tomography' to map the deformation shape.

ideas.description: This research direction integrates persistent homology from topological data analysis (TDA) into a Bayesian inference framework adapted from neutron star astrophysics. The method uses TDA to extract robust, multi-scale shape descriptors (e.g., Betti numbers, persistence diagrams) from the initial energy density distributions simulated by various models. These topological summaries are then used as high-level model outputs within a Bayesian hierarchical analysis, linking them to final-state observables like flow harmonics. This enables the joint posterior estimation of both the parameters governing the initial-state geometry and the underlying QCD transport properties (like shear viscosity), moving beyond traditional sequential tuning.

ideas.description: This approach moves beyond static topological description by applying persistent homology to the evolving energy density field during the pre-equilibrium phase of a heavy-ion collision, creating a time series of topological signatures. These dynamic shape descriptors are integrated with conventional physical parameters into a interpretable machine learning framework, such as a gradient-boosted tree or attention-based model. The system is trained to predict final-state observables like anisotropic flow coefficients and particle correlations, enabling a direct, quantitative assessment of which topological features are causally influential. This creates a robust, predictive link between the initial geometry's shape and the collision outcome, transforming topology from a characterization tool into a component of a predictive model.

ideas.title: Adapting Topographic Mapping to Define Nuclear Skin Deformation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the spherical assumption in nuclear skin definition by drawing an analogy from geophysics, where the spherical Earth model is relaxed using topographic mapping. In geophysics, a reference ellipsoid is defined, and deviations (topography) are mapped using various probes like satellite gravimetry. Adapted to nuclear physics, we propose to define a reference deformed shape (e.g., an ellipsoid with quadrupole deformation) as the baseline. The 'deformed skin'—where neutron skin thickness varies with the nuclear orientation relative to an incident probe—is then described as a thickness function relative to this baseline. This orientation-dependent skin can be probed by analyzing reaction cross-sections and charge radii from scattering experiments (e.g., proton-nucleus scattering) performed with polarized beams or aligned nuclei, creating a nuclear 'topographic map' that reveals how skin thickness depends on the viewing angle.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Signal Denoising to Isolate Flow Signatures in Heavy-Ion Collisions
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics, where ML models denoise seismic signals to isolate primary events from background scattering. The analogous problem in heavy-ion collisions is separating genuine collective flow signatures from nonflow correlations and background effects. We propose to train a deep learning model, such as a denoising convolutional neural network or a variational autoencoder, on high-fidelity simulated collision events where the true flow signal and background components are known and separable. The model will learn to generate an optimal event classifier or activity variable that functions as an adaptive filter, minimizing the influence of nonflow and background in experimental data, thereby purifying the flow signal for more precise physical analysis.

ideas.title: Global Bayesian Inference of Deformation Landscapes from Correlated Ratio Observables
ideas.description: This redesigned approach constructs a robust statistical framework to extract deformation parameters. Instead of inverting single ratios, it performs a joint Bayesian analysis of a global dataset of multiple electromagnetic and spectroscopic observables sensitive to quadrupole and octupole collectivity. The method uses predictions from a family of nuclear energy density functionals to establish physically motivated priors and likelihoods. The output is a complete posterior probability distribution for the deformation parameters, providing not only their most likely values but also rigorously quantified uncertainties and correlations, thereby turning an ill-posed inversion into a well-defined statistical mapping.

ideas.title: Decomposing Centrality Ratios via Signal Separation from a Non-Baseline Reference
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that centrality-based ratios serve primarily as a baseline or null expectation. Drawing an analogy from atmospheric science, where complex time series are decomposed into physically distinct modes (trend, seasonality, oscillations), we propose to treat centrality ratios as composite signals containing multiple physical effects. The analogous solution involves using signal separation techniques (e.g., blind source separation, matrix factorization) common in climate informatics. Adapted to this context, the research would apply such methods to decompose observed centrality ratios into orthogonal components, isolating the core centrality-dependent scaling from other superimposed phenomena like jet quenching, collective flow, or specific event topology effects. This shifts the analytical goal from subtracting a baseline to disentangling and identifying the distinct physical processes encoded within the ratio's structure.

ideas.title: Systematic-Agnostic Decomposition of Centrality Observables
ideas.description: This approach reframes centrality-dependent measurements by first constructing a comprehensive map of all known non-physical systematic effects (detector efficiency, geometry, model bias) as derived from simulation ensembles and data-driven techniques. The observed ratios are then projected onto a basis that separates variations aligned with these systematic modes from residual orthogonal components. The orthogonal residual is treated as the candidate physical signal, whose significance is tested against the null hypothesis of remaining unknown systematics using closure tests and ablated analysis models.
